findings.explanation: Ladle is a kitchen utensil, but it is not normally considered cutlery in the same everyday sense as fork, knife, and spoon. For an easy puzzle this makes the category feel imprecise.
findings.suggestedFix: Rename the group to "Kitchen Utensils" or replace Ladle with another clear cutlery item such as Teaspoon.

findings.explanation: Drill is usually a power tool, not a hand tool. The other items are straightforward hand tools, so this one weakens the category.
findings.suggestedFix: Replace Drill with Pliers, Screwdriver, or Chisel; or rename the group to "Tools".

findings.explanation: Footwear is a type of clothing/wear, so the labels "Clothing" and "Footwear" are semantically nested even though the listed items separate cleanly.
findings.suggestedFix: Rename "Clothing" to a narrower label such as "Upper-Body Clothing" or replace one category with a non-overlapping everyday category.

findings.explanation: "Board Games" and "Party Games" overlap as category concepts. Pictionary, Jenga, and Twister are commonly sold or described as board/tabletop games as well as party games, so the distinction is not clean for easy tier.
findings.suggestedFix: Use a more precise label such as "Classic Board Games" and choose party games that are not also commonly board/tabletop games, or replace one group.

findings.explanation: Spell is magical, but it is not an item in the same concrete sense as Wand, Potion, and Amulet. The label "Magical Items" is therefore imprecise.
findings.suggestedFix: Replace Spell with a concrete object such as Scroll, Ring, Crystal, or Talisman.

findings.explanation: Stracciatella and Neapolitan are less everyday than the rest of the batch, especially for an English easy puzzle. They are valid ice cream flavors, but the group is noticeably more specialized than expected.
findings.suggestedFix: Use more common flavors such as Chocolate, Strawberry, Mint, or Cookies and Cream.

findings.explanation: Building Materials overlaps with the more specific material categories implied by Cement, Concrete, Plaster, and Mortar. Concrete contains cement, and mortar often contains cement, making the item set internally related in a way that can feel like ingredients versus materials rather than four sibling examples.
findings.suggestedFix: For easy tier, use more distinct building materials such as Brick, Wood, Steel, and Concrete, or rename the category to "Masonry Materials" and avoid ingredient/product nesting.

findings.explanation: Iced Tea is tea and can be considered a hot drink served cold, while Chai and Matcha are also tea drinks. The hot/cold split relies on serving temperature rather than the drink identity, which can make Iced Tea feel connected to the Hot Drinks group.
findings.suggestedFix: Replace Iced Tea with Soda, Cola, Juice, or another cold drink without a hot-drink counterpart.

findings.explanation: Compass is ambiguous: it can be a drawing/measuring tool, but in an easy puzzle many players will think of a navigation compass rather than a measuring tool.
findings.suggestedFix: Replace Compass with Tape Measure, Protractor, or Square.

findings.explanation: Crescent and gibbous describe moon shape, while waxing and waning describe whether illumination is increasing or decreasing. They combine with each other rather than forming four parallel moon phases, so the category is not a clean set.
findings.suggestedFix: Use four full phase names such as New Moon, First Quarter, Full Moon, and Last Quarter, or use Waxing Crescent, Waxing Gibbous, Waning Crescent, and Waning Gibbous.

findings.explanation: Meteor and comet are sky objects/events, and a meteor shower or comet appearance can be a sky event, but the label mixes event types with objects. This is less concrete than expected for easy tier.
findings.suggestedFix: Use clearer event names such as Meteor Shower, Solar Eclipse, Lunar Eclipse, and Aurora.

findings.explanation: Contrail is not one of the standard high cloud genera alongside cirrus, cirrocumulus, and cirrostratus. Fog and mist are also not low cloud genera in the same way as stratus and stratocumulus.
findings.suggestedFix: Replace Contrail with a standard high-cloud term if available, and rename "Low Clouds" to include ground-level phenomena or use standard low-cloud types only.

findings.explanation: The cloud categories are nested/overlapping: fog is essentially cloud at ground level, mist is closely related to fog, and "Overcast" in Sky Colors describes cloud cover rather than a color. The puzzle mixes cloud types, weather signs, and sky conditions in a way that is not clean for easy tier.
findings.suggestedFix: Replace this puzzle or simplify it into concrete everyday groups such as Cloud Types, Storm Features, Weather Words, and Sky Colors with non-overlapping items.

findings.explanation: This is not easy-tier content. Gothic and Baroque architectural features, plus terms like bailey, stucco, rotunda, and glass curtain, require specialized architectural knowledge.
findings.suggestedFix: Move to a harder tier or replace with more everyday building categories.

findings.explanation: Glass Curtain is not a material; it refers to a curtain wall/system. The group label "Modernist Materials" is inconsistent with this item.
findings.suggestedFix: Use Glass, Steel, Concrete, and Plywood, or rename the group to "Modernist Architecture Features" and adjust the other items.

findings.explanation: Uranus and Neptune are ice giants, not gas giants under the common modern classification. The group label is factually shaky for an astronomy puzzle.
findings.suggestedFix: Rename the group to "Outer Planets" or use only Jupiter and Saturn in a different four-item category.

findings.explanation: This is more specialized than easy-tier English everyday knowledge. Programming paradigms and shell commands like Awk and Sed are developer-specific.
findings.suggestedFix: Move to a technical/easier-for-developers pool rather than general easy, or replace with more everyday computer terms.

findings.explanation: The puzzle depends on fairly specific German geography and culture. Items like Hesse, Thuringia, Main, Oder, and Spaetzle may be too regional for an English easy pool.
findings.suggestedFix: Move to medium or use more globally familiar German items.

findings.explanation: Fork is a chess tactic, not a special move. It also has an everyday meaning that does not fit the chess move category, making the group unfair.
findings.suggestedFix: Replace Fork with Check, Capture, or Kingside Castling; or rename the group to "Chess Tactics and Moves" and adjust the other items.

findings.explanation: Mezzo is not a complete dynamic marking by itself in the same way Piano and Forte are; it normally modifies piano or forte. Crescendo is also a dynamic change rather than a dynamic level. The category mixes related but non-parallel music terms.
findings.suggestedFix: Use Piano, Forte, Mezzo Piano, and Mezzo Forte, or rename the group to "Dynamic Markings" and keep parallel items.

findings.explanation: The puzzle is music-theory specific, with Italian tempo and articulation vocabulary. That is likely above general easy-tier expectation.
findings.suggestedFix: Move to medium or replace with everyday music categories like instruments, note values, or genres.

findings.explanation: Shepherd and Sheepdog are generic dog-type terms rather than clear breeds, and they overlap strongly with the herding category. Some Corgis are herding dogs but also widely treated as a small companion breed, making the group less clean.
findings.suggestedFix: Use specific breed names such as Border Collie, Australian Shepherd, Shetland Sheepdog, and Australian Cattle Dog.

findings.explanation: Sphynx is not a short-haired cat; it is commonly known as a hairless breed. It does not belong cleanly under "Short-haired Cats".
findings.suggestedFix: Replace Sphynx with Abyssinian, British Shorthair, Russian Blue, or American Shorthair.

findings.explanation: Pan and Dolly are camera movements, not camera shots in the same sense as Closeup and Wide. The group mixes shot sizes with movements.
findings.suggestedFix: Rename the group to "Camera Terms" or replace Pan and Dolly with Medium Shot and Long Shot.

findings.explanation: Theater overlaps with the other literary/form categories: tragedy and farce can be genres or forms, monologue is a script/performance element, and opera is a performing art rather than strictly theater in the same way. The label is broad and uneven.
findings.suggestedFix: Rename to "Stage Forms" and use clearer parallel items, or replace the group with a more concrete everyday category.

findings.explanation: Level appears under Masonry but is also a general measuring/toolbox item and appears as a measuring tool in similar contexts. It is not masonry-specific enough for an easy category.
findings.suggestedFix: Replace Level with Brick, Mortar, Trowel, or another masonry-specific item.

findings.explanation: Passport is a document and also a travel item in common puzzle vocabulary. Within this puzzle it is only in Documents, but the category is very broad and overlaps with other paper/document-like groups such as Mail Items and Printed Books.
findings.suggestedFix: Use narrower labels such as "Travel Documents", "Postal Items", and "Book Types", or replace broad document items with cleaner examples.

findings.explanation: Compass appears both as a map feature phrase (Compass Rose) and as a hiking gear item, and Scale is also a map feature tied to measuring. The map/hiking categories are close enough that Compass especially creates a near-duplicate cue.
findings.suggestedFix: Replace Compass in Hiking Gear with Boots, Trekking Poles, or Backpack; consider replacing Trail Map as well to keep the categories distinct.

findings.explanation: Lantern appears under Camping Gear while Headlamp appears under Hiking Gear; both are portable outdoor lighting gear and could reasonably be grouped together by players.
findings.suggestedFix: Use hiking-specific items such as Boots, Trekking Poles, Daypack, and Water Flask, and camping-specific items without overlapping lighting.

findings.explanation: Sashimi is commonly associated with sushi restaurants, but it is not sushi because it is sliced fish without vinegared rice. Under "Sushi Types" this is imprecise for a strict grouping puzzle.
findings.suggestedFix: Replace Sashimi with Uramaki, Gunkan, or Inari.

findings.explanation: The history categories are broad and require more specialized knowledge than easy tier, especially Assyria, Carthage, Medici, and distinctions among modern events.
findings.suggestedFix: Move to medium or simplify to more concrete school-level categories.

findings.explanation: Dante is usually classified as a late medieval writer rather than a Renaissance figure, and Columbus is transitional/early modern rather than a straightforward Renaissance figure. The group is debatable.
findings.suggestedFix: Use clearer Renaissance figures such as Michelangelo, Leonardo, Raphael, and Galileo, or rename the group to "Historical Figures".

findings.explanation: Face parts and sensory organs overlap conceptually because eyes, ears, tongue, and skin-related receptors are sensory anatomy. Retina and taste bud are also body parts in the broader sense. The anatomy labels are too nested for easy tier.
findings.suggestedFix: Use non-overlapping concrete groups such as Face Parts, Hand Parts, Digestive Organs, and Bones.

findings.explanation: Retina, cochlea, taste bud, and nerve ending are not sensory organs in the everyday anatomical sense; they are parts or receptors within sensory systems.
findings.suggestedFix: Use Eye, Ear, Nose, and Tongue, or rename the group to "Sensory Anatomy".

findings.explanation: Sands are soil/mineral materials and overlap conceptually with "Soil Types". The labels are not cleanly separate for a grouping puzzle.
findings.suggestedFix: Replace Sands with a non-overlapping category, or rename/rework both groups around broader earth materials.

findings.explanation: Ice Events, Skating, and Snowboarding are all winter sport/event categories, and several labels are nested or uneven. Hockey is an ice sport, while Figure, Speed, Short Track, and Pairs are skating disciplines; Bobsleigh and Luge are ice-track sports rather than "ice events" in a parallel sense.
findings.suggestedFix: Use precise labels such as "Ice Hockey/Curling", "Sliding Sports", "Skating Disciplines", and "Snowboarding Events", with items that do not overlap.

findings.explanation: Relay is not inherently a sprint; relays can be different distances. It also belongs broadly to track events like the other running categories, so the sprint category is not clean.
findings.suggestedFix: Replace Relay with 60m or 4x100m Relay if the label is adjusted to "Sprint Events".

findings.explanation: This is culturally broad and seasonally debatable for easy tier. Some items are unfamiliar or not obviously tied to the listed season for a general English audience.
findings.suggestedFix: Move to medium or use more universally familiar holidays with clearer seasonal anchors.

findings.explanation: Carnival is not consistently a summer event; many major Carnivals occur before Lent in winter or early spring. Mooncake is a food associated with the Mid-Autumn Festival rather than itself a festival.
findings.suggestedFix: Replace Carnival with a clearly summer event and replace Mooncake with Mid-Autumn Festival.

findings.explanation: Galaxy types, black hole classes, and stellar life stages are more advanced astronomy than easy-tier everyday knowledge.
findings.suggestedFix: Move to medium or replace with more basic space categories such as planets, moons, missions, and constellations.

findings.explanation: Hubble is primarily a space telescope, not a mission in the same everyday sense as Apollo, Voyager, and Juno. It can be referred to as a mission, but the item is less parallel.
findings.suggestedFix: Replace Hubble with Cassini, Galileo, or Pioneer, or rename the group to "Spacecraft and Missions".

findings.explanation: Tram is a rail vehicle, but not usually a train type in the same way as steam train, bullet train, and maglev train. Unicycle is also not a bicycle type in the ordinary sense; it is a related cycle with one wheel.
findings.suggestedFix: Replace Tram with Commuter or Freight, and replace Unicycle with BMX, Cruiser, or Folding Bike.

findings.explanation: Latin Dances and Ballroom Dances overlap because many Latin dances are part of ballroom dance classifications. The labels are nested rather than clean sibling categories.
findings.suggestedFix: Use "Latin Ballroom Dances" and "Standard Ballroom Dances" as clearer sibling labels, or replace one category.

findings.explanation: Folk & World is a broad catch-all and overlaps conceptually with genre categories. Reggae is not normally folk, and Flamenco/Fado are regional styles, so the label is less single-concept than the others.
findings.suggestedFix: Rename to "World Music Styles" or replace Reggae with another folk/world item and keep the label consistent.

findings.explanation: Gravity and magnetism are fundamental interactions, but friction and tension are contact forces. The label "Physics Forces" is broad enough, yet the set may imply fundamental forces and is not parallel.
findings.suggestedFix: Rename to "Forces in Physics Class" or use four everyday forces such as Gravity, Friction, Tension, and Normal Force.

findings.explanation: Radio, micro, infrared, and ultraviolet are regions of the electromagnetic spectrum, not wave types in a general physics sense. "Micro" should more naturally be "Microwave".
findings.suggestedFix: Rename to "Electromagnetic Waves" and use Microwave instead of Micro.

findings.explanation: Salad greens, leafy herbs, sprouts, and cabbage family overlap conceptually as edible leafy plants. Kale, bok choy, savoy, and napa are also leafy greens; broccoli sprout belongs to the same plant family as cabbage. The labels are too nested for a fair easy puzzle.
findings.suggestedFix: Replace one or more plant groups with unrelated everyday categories, or make the labels very specific and non-overlapping.
